original_hypothesis.title: LZHelperSender.updateTVL uses entire contract ETH balance as LayerZero fee, enabling accidental or adversarial ETH burn/drain via donations
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: LZHelperSender.updateTVL sets MessagingFee(nativeFee=address(this).balance). Because the contract has a payable receive() function, anyone can donate ETH; the next updateTVL will spend the full balance as fees. This can either burn treasury ETH or cause unpredictable fee payment behavior and potential message failures (if the fee must match exact quoting behavior).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code path using full balance. It is exploitable without any privileged access: attackers can force the protocol to overpay fees (economic griefing) by donating ETH, or cause operational uncertainty about fee budgeting.

inventory_refs.function: updateTVL(uint256 vaultId, uint256 tvl, uint256 updateTime)

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lzSend(..., MessagingFee(address(this).balance, 0), ...) uses entire ETH balance as fee; contract has `receive() external payable {}`.

preconditions: LZHelperSender holds ETH (either funded by protocol or donated by third parties)
An authorized omniChainManager calls updateTVL (normal operation)
LayerZero endpoint accepts the provided fee and consumes it

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: uses full contract balance for fee
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: unbounded fee payment and griefable economics
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fee quoting, no max fee cap, no refund management beyond payable(this)

analysis_questions: Does LayerZero refund unused native fee to the specified refund address (here: address(this))? If so, does it still create accounting/availability issues?
Can an attacker force meaningful loss (e.g., if endpoint consumes full fee)?
Is there any owner function to sweep ETH from LZHelperSender (not shown) to manage balances?
validation_requirements: Review LayerZero OApp v2 fee semantics for overpayment and refunds
Fork-test updateTVL with donated ETH and measure net ETH spent vs refunded
Check operational scripts for how fees are intended to be supplied

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker donates ETH to LZHelperSender. Next TVL update spends the entire ETH as message fee, potentially burning protocol funds or creating unpredictable fee usage. Repeated donations force repeated overpayment/griefing.
prerequisites: Attacker has ETH to donate (can be small if causing operational confusion rather than loss)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Economic griefing; potential fee overpayment / treasury loss depending on LZ refund rules
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users relying on cross-chain TVL sync correctness/availability
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Code change to use quoted fee and cap; add sweep function

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests around fee payment logic and donation/grief scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor incoming ETH donations and subsequent fee spending

validation_result.mechanism: unbounded native fee via full balance send
validation_result.impact_summary: LZHelperSender.updateTVL sends the entire contract ETH balance as LayerZero messaging fee. Anyone can donate ETH via receive(), inflating fees and creating economic griefing. No fee quoting, no cap, and overridden _payNative removes standard safeguards.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The LZHelperSender.updateTVL function constructs MessagingFee(address(this).balance, 0) and passes it to _lzSend, meaning the entire native balance of the contract is offered as the LayerZero messaging fee. The contract has an open `receive() external payable {}` function, allowing anyone to send ETH to it. The _payNative override removes the standard OAppSender check that validates msg.value against the fee, enabling the contract to pay from its stored balance without any validation. While LayerZero v2 endpoints typically refund excess native fees to the refundAddress (here payable(address(this))), this pattern is still defective: (1) there is no fee quoting via _quote() to determine the actual required fee, (2) there is no maximum fee cap, (3) the entire balance is transiently sent to the endpoint creating unnecessary risk, (4) any donation by a third party inflates the fee paid, and (5) there is no sweep/rescue function to recover excess ETH. The TODO comment in the code ('// TODO: send event here') also suggests this function was not fully finalized.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The vulnerable code is clearly present in LZHelperSender.sol. The updateTVL function uses address(this).balance as the nativeFee parameter. The _payNative override bypasses the standard OApp msg.value validation. The receive() function is completely open with no access control.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Uses address(this).balance as the entire native fee for the LayerZero send. No fee quoting, no cap, and no protection against the balance being inflated by external donations.
Open receive function allows anyone to send ETH to the contract. This ETH will be consumed as LayerZero fee on the next updateTVL call.

Overrides the standard OAppSender._payNative which normally validates msg.value >= nativeFee. This override removes all validation, enabling the contract to spend its entire stored balance without checks.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker sends ETH to LZHelperSender via receive() (LZHelperSender.sol:33)
4. updateTVL constructs MessagingFee(address(this).balance, 0) - entire balance as fee (LZHelperSender.sol:84)
6. _payNative returns the amount without validation, bypassing OApp standard checks
7. endpoint.send{value: address(this).balance}(...) sends all ETH to LayerZero endpoint
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack path starts with any address sending ETH to the LZHelperSender contract via its open receive() function. On the next legitimate call to updateTVLInfo() by the OmnichainManagerNormalChain (which calls LZHelperSender.updateTVL), the function reads address(this).balance which now includes the donated ETH. This inflated balance is passed as MessagingFee.nativeFee to _lzSend. The overridden _payNative at line 44-46 simply returns the amount without any validation. The endpoint receives the full balance. While LayerZero typically refunds excess to the refundAddress (payable(address(this))), there is no guarantee, no cap enforcement, and no fee quoting to determine the actual required fee.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script (deploySharedContracts.ts) deploys LZHelperSender with just the LZEndpoint and owner address. No fee-related configuration is set during deployment. No operational scripts show how ETH is intended to be supplied to the contract for fee payment.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is valid: (1) Attacker sends ETH to LZHelperSender via plain transfer (receive() accepts it). (2) On the next authorized updateTVL call, MessagingFee(address(this).balance, 0) includes the donated ETH. (3) The entire inflated balance is sent to the LayerZero endpoint as the native fee. (4) While excess may be refunded, the pattern creates economic waste, gas overhead, and risk of loss if refund fails. Repeated donations force repeated overpayment. No sweep function exists to recover ETH independently of updateTVL.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The defect allows economic griefing against the protocol. An attacker can donate ETH to force overpayment of LayerZero fees. Even with refund mechanisms, this creates: (1) unnecessary gas costs for value transfer round-trips, (2) risk of permanent loss if endpoint refund fails, (3) operational uncertainty about fee budgeting, (4) no independent mechanism to recover donated ETH. The missing fee quoting means the protocol cannot predict or control messaging costs. This is a genuine code defect - the standard OApp pattern uses _quote() to determine exact fees, but this contract bypasses all such safeguards.
recommended_mitigations: Use _quote() to determine the exact required fee before calling _lzSend, and pass only the quoted amount plus a small buffer as the MessagingFee
Add a maximum fee cap to prevent spending more than a configurable limit per message
Add an onlyOwner rescue/sweep function to recover excess ETH from the contract
Remove the open receive() function or restrict it to only accept ETH from the endpoint (for refunds)
Restore the standard _payNative validation or implement equivalent checks
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LZHelperSender with a mock LZ endpoint. 2. Configure vault info and chain info. 3. Fund the contract with 0.01 ETH (normal operation). 4. From an attacker EOA, send 10 ETH to the contract. 5. Trigger updateTVL via the authorized omniChainManager. 6. Observe that the full 10.01 ETH is sent as nativeFee to the endpoint. 7. Verify refund behavior and measure net ETH lost.
uncertainties: LayerZero v2 endpoint refund behavior for excess native fees - the endpoint should refund excess to payable(address(this)), but the exact implementation and edge cases are in external code not provided in scope
Whether there are operational procedures that limit the ETH balance sent to this contract, mitigating the impact in practice
additional_testing_needed: Fork test with real LayerZero v2 endpoint to measure actual fee consumption vs refund for a TVL update message
Test with varying donated ETH amounts to measure net loss after refunds
Test behavior when contract has zero ETH balance (message failure scenario)
Test concurrent updateTVL calls to verify no race condition on balance spending
